text.fullText: UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 C.F.R. Part 121) EXECUTIVE SUMMARY These comments request that EPA define the phrase “Federal license or permit” in 40 C.F.R. Part 121. Recent action by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires Clean Water Act §401 certification for hydropower exemptions from licensing (“license exemptions”) and license surrenders, proceedings that do not issue a license or permit and do not authorize a discharge. Historically, FERC interpreted §401—consistent with its early precedent referred to in Order No. 106—to recognize that certification was not required for exemption proceedings because no federal permit issued.1 In 2024, the Commission reinterpreted §401 to apply to any Commission authorization associated with potential water impacts.2 After the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024), agencies may not expand statutory scope based on 1 “In addition, if no license is issued, there is no applicable section 401 requirement.” Exemption From All or Part of Part I of the Federal Power Act of Small Hydroelectric Power Projects With an Installed Capacity of Five Megawatts or Less, Order No. 106, 45 Fed. Reg. 76115, 76120 (Nov. 18, 1980). See also, Philip Leber, Clean Water Act Permits and Hydroelectric Projects: What Are the Requirements?, Hydro Review, Winter 1983, at 18. 2 Establishment of Categorical Reasonable Period of Time for Action on Requests for Water Quality Certification Under Section 401(a)(1) of the Clean Water Act and Clarifying Types of Hydroelectric Project Proceedings That May Require Water Quality Certification, Order No. 2023, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (Nov. 21, 2024). 1 policy preferences or ambiguity. The statutory text of §401 ties certification to issuance of a federal license or permit for a discharge.3 A proceeding determining that a §401 certification of a license is unnecessary does not satisfy that condition. EPA should therefore clarify that §401 certification applies only to discharge-authorizing permits. Absent clarification, federal and state agencies may effectively nullify congressionally created hydropower exemptions under PURPA by imposing licensing-equivalent procedures. 3 Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 144 S. Ct. 2244, 2265–68 (2024)(holding courts must exercise independent judgment in interpreting statutes and may not defer to agency interpretations merely because a statute is ambiguous). 2 UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 C.F.R. Part 121) Comments of Paul V. Nolan, Esq. I. Introduction These comments address a critical jurisdictional gap in the proposed Section 401 rule: the Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA” or “Agency”) has not defined what constitutes a “Federal license or permit” that triggers the need for Clean Water Act (“CWA”) §401 certification. The issue is now urgent. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) recently adopted a policy requiring §401 certification for certain proceedings that are neither licenses nor permits, including hydropower exemptions from licensing and license surrender approvals. See Establishment of Categorical Reasonable Period of Time for Action on Requests for Water Quality Certification under Section 401(a)(1) of the Clean Water Act and Clarifying Types of Hydroelectric Project Proceedings that May Require Water Quality Certification, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (2024). Prior to the 2024 FERC policy, some state certifying agencies had likewise required certifications even while acknowledging that §401 does not require them. The Vermont Agency of Natural Resources’ website for hydro 401 certification, for example, states that FERC does not require a §401 certification before acting on a FERC exemption application, but the Secretary deems certification necessary to fulfill obligations in the FERC process as a fish and wildlife resource agency.4 4 “FERC hydropower license. FERC does not require a §401 certification before acting on a FERC exemption application, but the Secretary deems certification 3 The proposed rule must clarify the statutory trigger for certification. Without clarification, the rule will facilitate unlawful expansion of federal authority and create inconsistent national administration of §401. II. The Statutory Structure of Section 401 Section 401(a)(1) applies only when a Federal agency issues a license or permit for an activity that may result in a discharge into navigable waters. The trigger is therefore not federal involvement and not federal jurisdiction. It is a federal authorization for a discharge. Section 401 is a discharge-based statute. It ensures compliance with water quality standards before federal pollution-authorizing permits issue. It is not a general environmental review mechanism and does not attach to jurisdictional determinations. The proposed rule repeatedly references “licenses and permits” but never defines them. That omission now has concrete regulatory consequences. III. Congress Created Hydropower Exemptions to Reduce Federal Licensing In 1978 Congress enacted the Public Utility Regulatory Policies Act (“PURPA”), amending the Federal Power Act (“FPA”). Congress determined that the federal hydropower licensing program itself had become a barrier to small renewable energy development. Prior to PURPA, any non-federal hydropower project within federal necessary to fulfill the Secretary’s obligations in the FERC process as a fish and wildlife resource agency” -- https://dec.vermont.gov/watershed/business- support/water-quality-certification-section-401 (last visited February 16, 2026). 4 jurisdiction required a full FERC license regardless of size. Congress found that the licensing regime prevented development of small hydroelectric generation at existing dams and historic mill sites. Congress therefore reduced federal regulation. PURPA did not create a simplified license. Congress instead created an exemption from licensing. An exemption is a jurisdictional withdrawal and a statutory determination that a project shall not be licensed. IV. The Legal Meaning of an Exemption A hydropower exemption is not a permit, not a license, does not authorize a discharge, and does not substitute for a license. It is Congress’s directive that licensing authority does not apply. The Clean Water Act was enacted in 1972 — six years before Congress created hydropower exemptions. When Congress later created exemptions, it did not amend §401 to include them. If §401 applies to an exemption, a statutory removal of licensing becomes a licensing requirement, reversing the statutory structure. V. FERC’s New Certification Policy FERC now requires §401 certification for proceedings associated with hydropower exemptions and license surrenders. 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (2024). The Commission states that authorizations potentially associated with water impacts require certification even where no license is issued. Requiring certification for an exemption converts the exemption process into a licensing process — the very result Congress enacted PURPA to avoid. 5 VI. The Requirement Is Contrary to PURPA’s Purpose Congress enacted hydropower exemptions to rapidly expand small renewable generation at existing dams. Conditioning an exemption on §401 certification restores the regulatory burden Congress intentionally removed and effectively nullifies the exemption statute. The proceeding designed to avoid licensing becomes functionally indistinguishable from licensing. This conflicts with national energy policy encouraging distributed renewable generation. VII. State Practice Demonstrates the Problem Some certifying agencies require certification despite acknowledging it is not required. Section 401 is thus used as a procedural intervention mechanism rather than a water-quality certification. Section 401 does not authorize states to impose certification requirements where no federal permit exists. VIII. Recommended Regulatory Clarification EPA should define the statutory trigger in 40 C.F.R. §121.1 as a federal authorization that itself authorizes a discharge of pollutants into waters of the United States. EPA should also clarify that §401 does not apply to hydropower licensing exemptions, license surrender approvals, jurisdictional determinations, or proceedings that do not authorize a discharge. 6 Additional Analysis: FERC’s Reinterpretation of Section 401 and Prior Practice -- Order No. 106 For decades prior to its 2024 certification policy, FERC’s practice recognized that §401 certification was not required for hydropower exemption proceedings because no federal license or permit issued. Contemporaneous commentary by a former attorney in the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Office of General Counsel explained that Clean Water Act §401 applies only where a federal license is issued and therefore does not apply to hydropower licensing exemptions because an exemption proceeding determines that no license is required. Philip Leber, Clean Water Act Permits and Hydroelectric Projects: What Are the Requirements?, Hydro Review, Winter 1983, at 18. This interpretation was consistent with the Commission’s exemption rulemaking, which stated that “if no license is issued, there is no applicable section 401 requirement.” Exemption From All or Part of Part I of the Federal Power Act of Small Hydroelectric Power Projects With an Installed Capacity of Five Megawatts or Less, Order No. 106, 45 Fed. Reg. 76,115, 76,120 (Nov. 18, 1980). The exemption proceeding determined the absence of licensing authority rather than the issuance of a federal permit. In its 2024 rulemaking, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130, the Commission reinterpreted §401 to apply broadly to Commission ‘authorizations,’ including exemptions and license surrender approvals. This reinterpretation rests on an expanded reading of §401 rather than on any statutory amendment. The Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo requires courts and agencies to apply the best reading of statutory text. Section 401(a)(1) applies when a federal agency issues a license or permit for an activity that may result in a discharge. An exemption proceeding does not issue a license, does not issue a permit, and does not 7 authorize a discharge. The same is true for orders approving a license’s surrender. Accordingly, requiring certification for an exemption is inconsistent with the statutory text and converts a congressional withdrawal of licensing jurisdiction into a federal permitting requirement. EPA should clarify the meaning of ‘license or permit’ to avoid interagency conflict and ensure consistent national application of §401. Hydropower licensing exemptions were enacted to reduce federal licensing and encourage small renewable generation. Requiring §401 certification contradicts both the Clean Water Act and the Federal Power Act and undermines PURPA’s purpose. Section 401 applies only to discharge-authorizing permits, not to proceedings determining that no federal license is required. EPA should define “Federal license or permit” to ensure consistent national administration of §401. Respectfully submitted, ____________________________________ Paul V. Nolan, Esq. 5515 17th Street Arlington, VA 22205-2722 Email: pvnppvndiver@gmail.com 8
